result: I keep thinking about the small quiet that lives between the refrigerator’s hum and the dog’s next sigh, a pocket of time so slight it doesn’t qualify as a moment, yet it keeps showing up. I’ve started collecting these almost-not-things: the hush before rain decides to fall, the tremble in a grocery store peach that means it will never ripen, the way my mother’s voicemail still says “Good afternoon” even though she’s been gone two winters. Scientists claim memory is stored in folds of tissue, but I find mine wedged in these interludes, like letters slipped behind library shelves. Sometimes I unwrap one at traffic lights and feel the whole car swell with an amber afternoon when I was eight and believed the woods behind the school hid a door to elsewhere; I can almost smell the chalk dust and the creek. Other nights, when the upstairs neighbor paces in circles—her steps soft like moths knocking—they line up like dominoes and I understand they were never gaps at all but stitches, the thread pulling every hour I’ve lived into the single, ongoing fabric I keep mistaking for a life. Eventually the fridge clicks off, the dog exhales, and I realize the quiet isn’t absence; it’s glue. Tomorrow I’ll gather more: the pause in the barista’s tattoo when she almost misspells my name again, the streetlight’s blink that misses one heartbeat of darkness. I’m building an invisible quilt large enough to cover us all, though most people will never notice the warmth.

Scientists claim memory is stored in folds of tissue, but I find mine wedged in these interludes, like letters slipped behind library shelves. Sometimes I unwrap one at traffic lights and feel the whole car swell with an amber afternoon when I was eight and believed the woods behind the school hid a door to elsewhere; I can almost smell the chalk dust and the creek. Other nights, when the upstairs neighbor paces in circles—her steps soft like moths knocking—they line up like dominoes and I understand they were never gaps at all but stitches, the thread pulling every hour I’ve lived into the single, ongoing fabric I keep mistaking for a life. Eventually the fridge clicks off, the dog exhales, and I realize the quiet isn’t absence; it’s glue. Tomorrow I’ll gather more: the pause in the barista’s tattoo when she almost misspells my name again, the streetlight’s blink that misses one heartbeat of darkness. I’m building an invisible quilt large enough to cover us all, though most people will never notice the warmth.
